title: Detection rule severity distribution balance
spl:
`notable`
| stats count by urgency, rule_name
| sort urgency, - count
description: Shows concentration of CRITICAL/HIGH notables to balance analyst load.
value: Shows concentration of CRITICAL/HIGH notables to balance analyst load.
implementation: Normalize field names to your Splunk ES version and index names. Use summary indexing or ES introspection where `detection_metrics` is not present. For YARA, deploy a scripted input or HEC to ingest scanner JSON.
knownFalsePositives: Severity mix shifts when we tune noise, on-board new data, or reclassify categories — the distribution changes without a new incident if the process is expected.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security / custom.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Splunk ES notable events (`` `notable` `` macro).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Normalize field names to your Splunk ES version and index names. Use summary indexing or ES introspection where `detection_metrics` is not present. For YARA, deploy a scripted input or HEC to ingest scanner JSON.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
`notable`
| stats count by urgency, rule_name
| sort urgency, - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Detection rule severity distribution balance** — Shows concentration of CRITICAL/HIGH notables to balance analyst load.

Documented **Data sources**: Splunk ES notable events (`` `notable` `` macro). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security / custom. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Detection type** for this use case: Operational metrics — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Invokes macro `notable` — in Search, use the UI or expand to inspect the underlying SPL.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by urgency, rule_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Reconcile the distribution to **ES Incident Review** severities/urgency for a sample day so the chart is not just a label quirk in your data.



Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Single value, Time chart

Scripted input (generic example)
This use case relies on a scripted input. In the app's local/inputs.conf add a stanza such as:

```ini
[script://$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/YourApp/bin/collect.sh]
interval = 300
sourcetype = your_sourcetype
index = main
disabled = 0
```

The script should print one event per line (e.g. key=value). Example minimal script (bash):

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Output metrics or events, one per line
echo "metric=value timestamp=$(date +%s)"
```

For full details (paths, scheduling, permissions), see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md
grandmaExplanation: We balance how many critical versus low alerts we generate so the queue stays usable and the right issues pop out.
